metadata.seed: I mean, I'm a photographer, so I kind of. It's been stopping me a bit, my anxiety from being able to get work, and because every time there's an opportunity that is kind of involving me having to interact with people, I just tend to just turn it down. So I was kind of making things hard in that respect and just kind of friends wise and everything, I feel like I'm just kind of increasingly more alone. So it's kind of stopping me from making friends and, you know, like, relationships and stuff.

patient.query: I mean, I'm a photographer, so I kind of. It's been stopping me a bit, my anxiety from being able to get work, and because every time there's an opportunity that is kind of involving me having to interact with people, I just tend to just turn it down. So I was kind of making things hard in that respect and just kind of friends wise and everything, I feel like I'm just kind of increasingly more alone. So it's kind of stopping me from making friends and, you know, like, relationships and stuff.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not good enough to get work
I will embarrass myself
I will be rejected by others

entailment: Fear of being in a small group (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of anxiety whenever a new opportunity comes up, especially when it involves interacting with others. What evidence do you have that you’ll be rejected or embarrassed, and have there been moments when you felt confident or were welcomed instead? How might you test those thoughts by trying a small, low‑stakes interaction and observing what actually happens?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it does feel that way. When I think about new opportunities, my mind just jumps to all the ways it could go wrong, like what if I say something stupid or they don't like me? But I also remember some moments when I felt... okay, like when I joined that book club last year and actually ended up enjoying it, even though I was so nervous at first. I just don't know how to shake off that fear of messing up. Testing those thoughts seems tough;

automatic_thoughts: all the ways it could go wrong
what if I say something stupid
they don't like me

neutral: Fear of being in a small group (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re aware that your worries often feel overwhelming, yet you also have a memory of a time when you overcame that fear. What might be a small, low‑stakes situation where you could gently test the idea that you’ll say something stupid? How could you notice what actually happens in that moment?

patient.query: I guess I could try talking to someone new at the coffee shop or maybe just make small talk with a coworker in the break room. It seems simple enough, but I still feel that knot in my stomach just thinking about it. I’d want to focus on how the other person reacts, like if they smile or seem interested, instead of just getting stuck in my head. It's hard to believe it could go well, though.

triggers: talking to someone new at the coffee shop
making small talk with a coworker in the break room
automatic_thoughts: I still feel that knot in my stomach
I’d want to focus on how the other person reacts
it's hard to believe it could go well

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of tension about how the conversation might go. What evidence do you have that it won’t go well? Have you had any experiences where a similar situation turned out better than you expected?

patient.query: Yeah, there's definitely tension. I guess I just assume the worst will happen. Like, I can remember times where I thought I’d totally embarrass myself, but it didn't turn out so bad. But then I also remember the times when I felt totally awkward or misread a situation, and that sticks with me harder. It’s like I focus more on those moments because they feel more real, you know? It makes me think I should just avoid it altogether.

automatic_thoughts: assume the worst will happen
I’ll totally embarrass myself
focus more on negative moments

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how the memories of feeling awkward or misreading situations seem to stand out more than the times things actually went okay. What evidence do you have that the worst outcome is likely, and how often do you notice the opposite? How might you view those “real” moments differently if you considered that they could also be just one part of a broader pattern?

patient.query: Yeah, there's definitely tension. I just assume the worst will happen. I can remember times I thought I’d totally embarrass myself, but it didn't turn out so bad. But then I think about those moments when I felt awkward or misread a situation, and those stick with me harder. I end up focusing more on those because they feel more real, you know? It makes me think I should just avoid it altogether.

I’ll totally embarrass myself
those moments stick with me harder
focus on negative experiences
think I should just avoid it

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that the worst will happen, yet you’ve already had moments where things didn’t turn out so badly. What evidence do you have that the worst outcome is inevitable? Could there be other ways to view those awkward moments that might lessen their impact?

patient.query: I guess when I imagine the worst, it feels more familiar somehow, like a safety net for my expectations. It’s easier to prepare for something bad than to hope for something good and get let down, you know? And those awkward moments, they feel so heavy in my mind, but maybe they’re just moments—maybe they don’t define me. Still, it’s hard to shake that feeling that I’ll just end up feeling stupid again.

automatic_thoughts: it's easier to prepare for something bad
hoping for something good leads to disappointment
those moments don't define me
I'll just end up feeling stupid again

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re using those “worst‑case” images as a kind of protective cushion. What evidence do you have that these imagined outcomes actually happen, and how often do they turn out to be true? If you could picture a different way of thinking about those awkward moments, how might that shift how you feel afterward?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s like I cling to those worst-case scenarios because they feel safer or more predictable. I mean, I can't think of a lot of times where things went as poorly as I imagined, but when they do turn out okay, it feels almost surprising. If I could look at those awkward moments differently, maybe I wouldn’t feel like I have to carry all that weight afterward. I guess the idea of just shrugging it off as a part of life sounds nice, but it's hard to

automatic_thoughts: cling to worst-case scenarios
things will turn out poorly
surprising positive outcomes
carrying all that weight

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It makes sense that having a clear, if negative, picture feels safer than uncertainty. What evidence do you have that the worst‑case scenarios actually happen more often than the more positive ones? If you could imagine a different way to view those awkward moments—perhaps as opportunities to learn or simply as normal parts of life—how might that shift the weight you feel afterward?

patient.query: I mean, I don’t really have solid evidence, just a lot of memories that feel vivid, you know? It’s like those awkward moments play on repeat in my head, and I can’t seem to shake them. Thinking of them as opportunities feels... kind of nice, but also really daunting. It’s hard to imagine feeling lighter after those moments when I usually fixate on them for ages. I want to believe I could just let them go, but I’m not sure how to start

automatic_thoughts: those moments play on repeat
I can't seem to shake them
thinking of them as opportunities feels daunting
I usually fixate on them for ages
I want to believe I could just let them go

llm_response.response: It sounds like these memories feel stuck in a loop—what might happen if you tried to step back from them for a moment? What evidence do you have that they will stay the same no matter what you do? How might you view these moments if you imagined them as just one part of a larger story rather than the whole story?

patient.query: I think stepping back might feel... freeing, maybe? But it’s scary too, like if I look away, they might come right back and hit me harder. I guess I don’t have solid proof that they’ll always be the same; it just feels like they define me in moments when I’m already feeling vulnerable. If I tried to see them as just one part of my story, I could maybe breathe a little easier. But it’s tough to convince myself that they don’t tell the

automatic_thoughts: if I look away, they might come right back and hit me harder
they define me in moments when I’m already feeling vulnerable
I could maybe breathe a little easier

entailment: Fear of being left alone during period of dependence (finding)
neutral: Identification with the aggressor - mental defense mechanism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding a lot of uncertainty about what might happen if you step back. What evidence do you have that looking away will lead to them coming back harder? Can you think of any times when you stepped back and didn’t experience more harm, or when the situation unfolded differently than you feared? Exploring those moments might help you see a broader range of possibilities.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it's been a while since I really thought about those moments where things didn’t go so badly. There was that one time at a party when I felt nervous about talking to someone new, but I just... pushed through it, and we ended up laughing about something silly. I didn’t walk away feeling worse, and it was kind of nice. But then again, those good moments feel so fragile compared to the weight of my fears. It’s just so hard to trust that it

automatic_thoughts: things didn’t go so badly
good moments feel fragile
hard to trust

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing how quickly that positive moment feels like it could slip away. What signs or thoughts make you think those good moments are fragile? Are there any details that suggest they might be more lasting than they feel? How might you remind yourself of the resilience you showed by pushing through the nervousness?
